metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or merge into AC1) explicitly requiring that `docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md` (or `iam.tf`) notes the tag rename from `project=snap-demo` to `Project=rebar`.
findings.finding: The 'What' section includes a retag note (`project=snap-demo` → `Project=rebar`) as a deliverable, but no Acceptance Criterion maps to it, so an executor has no way to verify this item is done and it could be silently skipped.
findings.location: What section (last bullet: 'Note the retag…') vs. Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: '…including a note that the resource tag was renamed from `project=snap-demo` to `Project=rebar`.' Or add a fifth AC: 'Tag rename (`project=snap-demo` → `Project=rebar`) is documented in the relevant file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion verifying the retag note (e.g., 'At least one documentation artifact notes the resource tag change from `project=snap-demo` to `Project=rebar`') or explicitly fold the retag note into an existing AC with a named target file.
findings.finding: The What section describes a fifth deliverable — noting the retag from `project=snap-demo` to `Project=rebar` — but no Acceptance Criterion covers this item, making it an orphan deliverable with no verifiable done condition.
findings.location: What (last bullet) / Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: '…and notes the resource tag change from `project=snap-demo` to `Project=rebar`.' (assuming the retag note belongs in gerrit-aws-setup.md alongside the ownership update).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC requiring that `docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md` (or the runbook) documents the `project=snap-demo` → `Project=rebar` retag so the change is not silently omitted.
findings.finding: The retag change (`project=snap-demo` → `Project=rebar`) is listed in the 'What' section but absent from all four Acceptance Criteria, meaning it can be silently dropped without failing any AC check, leaving stale tag documentation.
findings.location: What section (retag bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fifth AC: '`docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md` or the SSO runbook notes the resource retag from `project=snap-demo` to `Project=rebar` and its rationale.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a sub-bullet in an existing AC) that verifies the retag documentation is present — e.g., 'docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md (or the ADR) notes the Project tag change from snap-demo to rebar.'
findings.finding: The 'What' section lists the retag notation (project=snap-demo → Project=rebar) as a deliverable, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies it, leaving it without an end-state-observable proof.
findings.location: What section (final bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: '- [ ] The re-homing ADR (or gerrit-aws-setup.md) records the retag from project=snap-demo to Project=rebar.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` reference from the SSO runbook deliverable — the sso-gate was explicitly excluded from the terraform adoption story (ticket 7246); replace it with the actual mechanism rebar does own (e.g., 'how to add a subdomain to the existing CloudFront/Lambda `auth_host` stack in `auth_host.tf`') or drop the subdomain-protection section if it is out of scope for this docs ticket.
findings.finding: The What section instructs the SSO runbook to cover 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module', describing it as an already-existing module. But the `sso-gate` module does not exist anywhere in the repo (grep returns zero results), and the parent terraform adoption ticket (7246) explicitly excludes it: 'Does NOT adopt snap's ... audit-dashboard sso-gate.' The runbook would document a non-existent abstraction with zero call-sites — a YAGNI violation.
findings.location: What section, SSO runbook bullet: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module' with 'how to add a new subdomain to rebar's adopted `aws_cloudfront_distribution.auth_host` stack (see `infra/terraform/auth_host.tf`)' — or remove the bullet entirely and note it as a future work item once a reusable module is actually built.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` will exist (landed or landing simultaneously) before the runbook references it as 'an existing module', or scope the subdomain-protection section to a follow-up once the terraform story lands.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the SSO runbook will reference 'an existing module' — specifically `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` — but no `infra/terraform/modules/` directory exists in the repo and no `sso-gate` reference appears anywhere in the codebase. This makes the runbook's 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via sso-gate' section unwritable as specified unless the module is created first (which the plan scopes to the terraform adoption story, not this doc story).
findings.location: What section — SSO runbook bullet: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit sequencing constraint: 'This story MUST NOT merge until the terraform adoption story (which creates infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate) is merged.' Alternatively, stub the runbook section with a TODO referencing the terraform story's ticket, so the doc is honest about the module's pending state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or correct the claim that `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` will exist: the sibling terraform story explicitly does NOT adopt the sso-gate module, so the runbook should either omit the sso-gate subdomain-protection section or replace it with the actual adopted pattern (auth_sso.tf / auth_host.tf usage).
findings.finding: The plan states the SSO runbook will document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host.' But `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` does not exist in the repo, and the sibling terraform story (7246-bd9e-58a3-41fd) explicitly excludes it: 'Does NOT adopt snap's github_deploy role, audit-dashboard sso-gate.' The plan's assertion that sso-gate will exist at implementation time is contradicted by the dependency ticket's stated scope.
findings.location: What section — SSO runbook bullet: 'infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the sso-gate runbook section with a description of how the auth_host + API GW proxy pattern (as actually adopted in auth_host.tf/auth_sso.tf) can be extended to protect a new subdomain, since that is what the terraform story delivers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the sso-gate module dependency: either (a) add an explicit AC that this story is blocked until infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate lands in the repo, or (b) rewrite the runbook bullet to describe the planned module path conditionally ('once the terraform adoption story lands, protect a new subdomain via…') so the doc story can ship independently without asserting a non-existent artifact exists.
findings.finding: The What section asserts the SSO runbook will reference 'an existing module' at infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate, but no such module exists anywhere in the repo (infra/terraform/ has no modules/ subdirectory; all sso-gate searches return zero hits). The Scope clause hedges with 'Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside)', but the parenthetical '— which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module' treats the module's existence as a resolved fact. This is an unverified capability assertion: if the terraform story hasn't landed, the runbook section describing how to use sso-gate will reference a non-existent artifact.
findings.location: What section, SSO operations runbook bullet: '...how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the runbook bullet to: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module (at infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate, adopted in the companion terraform story — verify that story has merged before following this section)' and add an AC: 'The SSO runbook's sso-gate section is gated on / validated against the actual module landing in infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a gating pre-condition to AC4 (or a runbook authoring note) requiring that the `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module exists in the repo before the subdomain-protection section is written, OR specify that the runbook section be stubbed with a clear 'pending terraform adoption story' placeholder until the dependency lands.
findings.finding: The runbook AC specifies documenting 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module', but no such module exists anywhere in the repo (infra/terraform/ has no modules/ subdirectory), and the plan's own Scope section says it 'Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside)'. The runbook AC contains no conditional guard — if the adoption story has not yet landed when this doc story is implemented, the runbook will describe a Terraform workflow referencing a nonexistent module path, making the runbook non-reproducible as-code and factually incorrect at the time of writing.
findings.location: What section (runbook bullet) + Acceptance Criteria AC4: 'An infra/runbooks/ SSO runbook covers architecture, secrets, rotation, and incident revoke.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC or the 'What' bullet, add: 'If the terraform adoption story has not yet landed at merge time, the subdomain-protection section of the runbook MUST be marked [PENDING: sso-gate module not yet in repo] rather than referencing a nonexistent module path.' This makes the dependency explicit and prevents a misleading runbook from shipping.

findings.finding: The runbook AC requires documenting 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module', but no `sso-gate` module exists anywhere in the repo (searches for both `sso-gate` and `sso_gate` return zero hits). The runbook will either document a nonexistent module or the AC silently depends on that module being created/adopted in the companion terraform story — neither case is addressed in this plan.
findings.location: What section — SSO operations runbook bullet; AC line 4 ('An `infra/runbooks/` SSO runbook covers architecture, secrets, rotation, and incident revoke')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'The SSO runbook's sso-gate section is gated on the sso-gate module landing in the terraform adoption story; stub that section with a TODO if the module is not yet merged.' Alternatively, if sso-gate is a snap artifact being adopted, name it explicitly and confirm its HCL path.

findings.finding: The 'What' section specifies the SSO runbook should cover 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module', but no sso-gate module exists anywhere in the repo, and the parent terraform adoption ticket (7246) explicitly excludes it: 'Does NOT adopt snap's ... audit-dashboard sso-gate'. The runbook would be documenting a non-existent module.
findings.location: What section, SSO runbook bullet: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module' with 'how to add a new rebar subdomain to the existing auth_host CloudFront/Lambda SSO stack' — or remove the bullet entirely if that procedure is out of scope for this docs ticket.

findings.finding: The runbook's 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module' section references a Terraform module that has no confirmed existence in rebar's infra/terraform tree. Adapting this section verbatim from snap's RUNBOOK.md without verifying the module exists in rebar constitutes cargo-cult documentation — operators following the runbook would encounter a dead reference.
findings.location: What section — 'Add an SSO operations runbook under infra/runbooks/ … how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module'

findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section directs the runbook author to document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module', but no `sso-gate` Terraform module exists anywhere in infra/terraform/** (or elsewhere in the repo). The runbook would be referencing a non-existent module.
findings.location: What section: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module'
findings.suggested_fix: If the sso-gate pattern is the auth_host Lambda+CloudFront pattern from snap, name it concretely in the runbook outline (e.g., 'reuse the `snap-demo-auth-host` CloudFront+Lambda authorizer pattern') rather than implying a standalone Terraform module called `sso-gate`.

findings.finding: The What section references 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module' as a required runbook section, but no `sso-gate` module exists anywhere in `infra/terraform/` per the directory listing. This is an unverified capability assertion — the module may not yet exist at the time this documentation ticket is executed.
findings.location: What section — 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module'; Acceptance Criteria AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit dependency note: 'sso-gate module runbook section requires the terraform adoption story to land first' and either defer that subsection to a follow-on ticket or confirm the module's inputs/outputs are available for documentation.

findings.finding: The plan's runbook requirement states it should cover 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module,' but no `sso-gate` module exists anywhere in the repository. A full-repo grep for 'sso-gate' returns zero matches.
findings.location: What section: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the sso-gate module'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) make this story formally blocked by the terraform adoption story (so `sso-gate` is guaranteed to exist when the docs land), or (b) rewrite the sso-gate runbook section as explicitly conditional/pending (e.g. 'once `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` is adopted — see [terraform story] — use it as follows…') rather than asserting it as already existing.
findings.finding: The runbook's section on 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module' embeds a hidden ordering assumption: the plan asserts the module will exist because 'the terraform story adopts [it] into rebar as code alongside the auth host,' but the Scope only says this story 'Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside).' No enforcement mechanism prevents this documentation story from landing first. `infra/terraform/modules/` does not exist in the repo today. If the two stories land out of order, the runbook's most operationally critical section (how to onboard a new subdomain) will reference a non-existent module path from the moment the docs land, making the instruction broken rather than merely incomplete.
findings.location: What — SSO runbook bullet ('how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module'); Scope ('Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside)')
findings.suggested_fix: In the SSO runbook's 'protect a new subdomain' section, frame the sso-gate instructions with a conditional header: 'Prerequisites: the `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module must be adopted (terraform adoption story). Until then, contact the infra team for manual onboarding.' This makes the dependency explicit in the artifact itself, surviving any ordering slip.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit merge-order gate: specify that this doc story must not merge until `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` exists in the repo (i.e., the terraform adoption story has landed), or scope the sso-gate runbook section to a follow-on story once the module is confirmed present.
findings.finding: The 'What' section asserts the SSO runbook will reference 'the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module' as 'an existing module,' but no `infra/terraform/modules/` directory — let alone an `sso-gate` submodule — exists anywhere in the repo. The plan hedges with 'the terraform story adopts [it] into rebar as code … alongside the auth host,' but the Scope says it 'depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside),' meaning concurrent landing is acceptable. If this doc story merges before the terraform story lands, the runbook will contain a dangling module reference that operators cannot actually use — a capability gap at the point of publication.
findings.location: What section — SSO operations runbook bullet: 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add an AC: 'The `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module exists in the repo before this story is merged,' enforcing the dependency; or (b) mark the sso-gate runbook section as a stub/placeholder with a TODO pointing at the terraform story's ticket, so the runbook is honest about its completeness state at merge time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC4 to enumerate the required runbook sections: architecture, secrets (`/auth-solutions/*`), cookie-key rotation, Google-secret rotation, incident revoke, and sso-gate onboarding for new subdomains.
findings.finding: AC4 requires the runbook to 'cover architecture, secrets, rotation, and incident revoke' but omits two content items called out in the What section ('cookie-key rotation' vs 'Google-secret rotation' as distinct rotations, and 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable sso-gate module'), making the AC under-specified relative to the stated scope.
findings.location: What section (SSO runbook bullet) vs. AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC4 as: 'An `infra/runbooks/` SSO runbook exists with sections covering: (1) architecture, (2) secrets under `/auth-solutions/*`, (3) cookie-key rotation procedure, (4) Google OAuth secret rotation procedure, (5) incident revoke lever, (6) how to onboard a new rebar subdomain via `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate`.  Verify with: `ls infra/runbooks/sso*.md` (file exists) and manual section-heading check.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm with the parent epic whether the sibling terraform story is expected to produce a reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module; if not, remove or rephrase the runbook's 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via sso-gate' section so the leaf stays within the parent's defined scope of documenting the adopted 18 resources.
findings.finding: The leaf's runbook deliverable includes documenting 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module,' but the parent epic's plan describes adopting 18 existing shared resources (OIDC provider, wildcard cert, auth_host SSO stack) and has no Success Criterion or AC specifying creation of a reusable sso-gate Terraform module or a new-subdomain onboarding pattern. The leaf is asserting an artifact and a documentation scope that the parent does not contain.
findings.location: Leaf What section — SSO runbook bullet; parent epic SC1 and AC structure
findings.suggested_fix: If the sso-gate module is a genuine sibling deliverable, add it to the parent epic's Success Criteria (e.g., 'A reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module is adopted and documented') so the leaf's runbook reference is parent-backed. If it is not, narrow the runbook bullet to 'architecture, secrets, rotation, and incident revoke for the adopted auth_host stack' — matching exactly what the parent's SC5 and the leaf's own AC4 specify.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify ordering in Scope: either (a) assert this story MUST land after the terraform adoption story and remove 'or landing alongside', or (b) acknowledge the module may not yet exist and note the runbook reference should be added/verified as a follow-up once the module lands.
findings.finding: The What section states the runbook will reference the sso-gate module as 'an existing module' (implying it exists at implementation time), but the Scope states this story 'Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside)' — meaning this doc story could land before the module exists, contradicting the runbook's premise.
findings.location: Scope / What (runbook bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Change Scope to: 'Documentation only. Depends on the terraform adoption story landing first; the runbook's sso-gate module reference should be verified against the adopted module path before merging.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicit merge-ordering gate) requiring that `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` exists in the repo before the SSO runbook that references it is considered complete, or specify that the runbook must include a conditional note if the module has not yet landed.
findings.finding: The runbook AC requires coverage of the `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module, but the plan explicitly states it 'depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside)' with no AC guard to ensure the module actually exists before the runbook is merged — leaving the runbook with a potentially forward-referencing, unverifiable path.
findings.location: Scope section; AC: 'An `infra/runbooks/` SSO runbook covers architecture, secrets, rotation, and incident revoke'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'The `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module exists at `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate/` (or the runbook explicitly marks the sso-gate section as pending the terraform adoption story).' Alternatively, add a merge-dependency note in the Scope: 'This story must not close until the terraform adoption story has landed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC2, e.g.: `grep -c 'assumes it exists\|already exists in the account' infra/terraform/iam.tf` should return `0` after the fix.
findings.finding: No AC has a concrete proving command; for AC2 especially, the plan should name a grep or diff check so an executor (or reviewer) can deterministically verify the old 'assumes it exists' comment is gone from `infra/terraform/iam.tf`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: "`infra/terraform/iam.tf`'s OIDC comment no longer claims the provider is externally owned."
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'Verify with: `grep -c "already exists in the account\|assumes it exists" infra/terraform/iam.tf` → must return 0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC with a concrete validation mechanism — e.g., 'A team member not familiar with the SSO stack can follow the runbook's rotation section without needing to consult snap's repo' or 'All ARNs/secret paths in the runbook are verified against the live AWS account.'
findings.finding: All four Acceptance Criteria are pure existence checks ('documents X,' 'covers Y,' 'records Z') with no concrete validation mechanism — no before/after workflow comparison, no 'an on-call engineer can execute step N,' and no other operational signal confirming the docs are accurate and usable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four bullets)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture of the incident revoke lever in the runbook spec: state who can invoke it (IAM role / SSM-privileged operator / AWS console), what authentication mechanism gates it, and whether it is internal-only or externally reachable.
findings.finding: The SSO runbook's 'incident revoke lever' section has no declared access posture — the plan does not state who can invoke the lever, via what mechanism, or with what authentication. For a public-internet-facing SSO boundary, an undeclared operational access posture on the revoke path is a T5c gap (ambiguity about access level is itself the failure).
findings.location: What section — 'Add an SSO operations runbook … incident revoke lever'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the runbook AC or What section: 'The incident revoke lever section must declare its access class (e.g. internal-only: requires AWS SSM Session Manager access to the host, or requires IAM privilege on /auth-solutions/* SSM path) and must not be reachable by an unauthenticated actor.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare in the runbook spec (or SSO operations section of the plan) that all /auth-solutions/* parameters holding secrets (Google OAuth client secret, cookie key) must be SSM SecureString, consistent with the /rebar/prod/* model established in ADR-0008.
findings.finding: The plan references 'secrets /auth-solutions/*' for the SSO stack (Google OAuth client secret, cookie key) but never declares whether these parameters are stored as SSM SecureString (encrypted at rest) or String (plaintext). For a public-internet-facing SSO boundary carrying OAuth client secrets and session cookie keys, the at-rest protection type is a load-bearing secret-lifecycle declaration that is absent from the plan.
findings.location: What section — 'Add an SSO operations runbook … secrets /auth-solutions/*'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section or AC-4: 'The runbook must state that /auth-solutions/* secret parameters (cookie key, Google client secret) are SecureString type, encrypted with the AWS-managed SSM KMS key, and must include the --type SecureString flag in any rotation commands shown.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
